name: scanning-tools
description: This skill should be used when the user asks to \"perform vulnerability scanning\", \"scan networks for open ports\", \"assess web application security\", \"scan wireless networks\", \"detec...
capabilities: Execute network discovery scans using Nmap and Masscan with appropriate techniques
Configure and run vulnerability scanners including Nessus and OpenVAS
Perform web application security testing with Burp Suite and OWASP ZAP
Conduct wireless network security assessments using Aircrack-ng suite
Generate compliance reports for CIS, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA frameworks
Produce professional security reports in multiple output formats
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analyzer flagged 184 patterns without semantic context. All findings are false positives - this is educational documentation containing bash command examples for authorized security testing. The skill explicitly requires written authorization and follows responsible disclosure practices. Risk factors are inherent to security tool documentation, not malicious intent.
valueStatement: Security professionals need comprehensive guidance on using scanning tools effectively and legally. This skill provides structured documentation for network discovery, vulnerability assessment, and compliance validation using industry-standard tools.
userTitle: Master Security Scanning Tools for Authorized Testing
limitations: Requires proper authorization before scanning any systems not owned by the user
Cannot execute actual scanning tools - provides documentation and guidance only
Does not replace certified penetration testing tools or professional security assessments
Some advanced features require commercial licenses (Nessus Professional, Burp Suite Pro)

useCases.title: Internal Security Assessment
useCases.description: Security teams performing authorized internal network assessments can use this skill to plan and execute comprehensive vulnerability scans with proper documentation.

useCases.title: Compliance Validation
useCases.description: IT auditors and compliance officers can leverage scanning tools to validate security controls against regulatory requirements like PCI-DSS and HIPAA.

useCases.title: Web Application Testing
useCases.description: Development teams can integrate security scanning into their CI/CD pipelines to identify vulnerabilities before production deployment.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Network Scan
promptTemplates.prompt: Scan the internal network 192.168.1.0/24 for live hosts and open ports. Use appropriate timing to avoid triggering alerts.
promptTemplates.scenario: Initial network reconnaissance for authorized internal assessment

promptTemplates.title: Web Application Security Scan
promptTemplates.prompt: Perform a comprehensive security scan of https://example-app.com using OWASP ZAP. Focus on OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities and generate an HTML report.
promptTemplates.scenario: Pre-deployment security validation for web applications

promptTemplates.title: Vulnerability Assessment Report
promptTemplates.prompt: Run Nessus vulnerability scan against the target subnet and generate a PDF report with executive summary, risk distribution, and remediation recommendations.
promptTemplates.scenario: Quarterly vulnerability assessment for compliance requirements

promptTemplates.title: Wireless Security Audit
promptTemplates.prompt: Document the process for auditing wireless network security including monitor mode setup, network discovery, and security protocol validation.

bestPractices: Always obtain written authorization before scanning any systems you do not own
Start with passive reconnaissance before active scanning to minimize impact
Document all scanning activities and maintain audit trails for compliance
antiPatterns: Scanning systems without explicit written permission - this is illegal in most jurisdictions
Using aggressive scan timing on production systems - can cause service disruption
Ignoring false positive validation - always manually verify critical findings before reporting

faq.question: Do I need special permission to use these scanning tools?
faq.answer: Yes, you must have explicit written authorization from the system owner before scanning. Unauthorized scanning may violate computer crime laws.

faq.question: Can this skill actually execute security scans?
faq.answer: This skill provides documentation and guidance. You must have the actual tools installed (Nmap, Nessus, etc.) and execute commands in your terminal.

faq.question: What is the difference between passive and active scanning?
faq.answer: Passive scanning observes network traffic without sending probes. Active scanning sends packets to targets and may trigger security alerts.

faq.question: How do I avoid false positives in vulnerability scans?
faq.answer: Use credentialed scanning when possible, manually verify critical findings, and correlate results from multiple tools before reporting.

faq.question: Which scanning tool is best for web applications?
faq.answer: Burp Suite Professional and OWASP ZAP are industry standards. Burp offers more advanced features; ZAP is free and open-source.

faq.question: How often should I run vulnerability scans?
faq.answer: For compliance (PCI-DSS), quarterly scans are required. Best practice is continuous scanning with each significant change to infrastructure.
